name: National Renaissance; Early Uprising Against British Rule

sourceRefs.label: 2025_q1 page 72 Q132 (chronological order of uprisings, with answer key)
2026_q page 93 Q113 (Raja Ram Mohan Roy's philosophy quote, with answer key)
tnpscguru.in - 21-question real past-paper set on National Renaissance & socio-religious reform movements

andedge.com, weshineacademy.com - Prarthana Samaj, Theosophical Society, Aligarh Movement, Young Bengal Movement, Satyashodhak Samaj, Singh Sabha, and Tamil Nadu reformers (Vallalar, Vaikunda Swamigal) rounding out the all-India + TN reform-movement roster
2024_q page 3 Q1 (revolt-area matching: Ramosis/Kuka/Santhals/Kittur, with answer key)
pmfias.com, edukemy.com, prepp.in - Wahabi Movement, Faraizi Movement, Kuka Movement, Santhal Rebellion, Titu Mir, Kol Uprising, Paika Rebellion, Kittur Rebellion (pre-1857 early uprisings)
embibe.com, vajiramandravi.com - Veda Samaj (Madras), Pandita Ramabai, Deoband Movement

researchNotes: 2 direct real hits plus an unusually large 21-question web-sourced set. Picked ~16 of the clearest, highest-yield facts (founders, dates, signature quotes/slogans) and dropped some of the more obscure ones (exact newspaper mottos, minor association founders) to keep the set high-value rather than padded. One sourced fact (Widow Remarriage Act 1856 attributed to 'Lord Dalhousie') is flagged — historically the Act was passed in July 1856, after Dalhousie's term had already ended (Feb 1856) and Lord Canning's had begun, so 'Canning' is the more standard answer in many references; presented with this caveat rather than silently accepted. Expanded 2026-07 pass: re-scanned OCR (no new genuine hits beyond the 2 already used — a widow-remarriage-scheme hit on 2026_q page 17 turned out to be a current welfare scheme, not the 1856 Act, so correctly excluded), then added 8 more reform-movement organizations/reformers (Prarthana Samaj, Theosophical Society, Aligarh Movement, Young Bengal Movement, Satyashodhak Samaj, Vidyasagar's specific role, TN reformers Vallalar/Vaikunda Swamigal, Singh Sabha) since the original pass, while accurate, under-covered the syllabus's full reform-movement roster. Round-2 pass (2026-07, second expansion): re-scanned OCR specifically for 'early uprising' keywords (Santhal/Wahabi/Kuka/etc.) and found a genuine new hit — 2024_q page 3 Q1, a real 'revolt-area matching' question covering Ramosis/Kuka/Santhals/Kittur, incorporated as both a reading item (r33) and two mock questions (m31 correct-pair version, m32 wrong-pair-flip trap) so both phrasings are covered. Added 6 more pre-1857 armed uprisings the topic's 'Early Uprising' half had not yet covered (Wahabi, Faraizi, Kuka, Santhal Hul, Titu Mir, Kol Uprising, Paika Rebellion, Kittur Rebellion) plus 2 more renaissance-side reformers (Veda Samaj, Pandita Ramabai) and the Deoband Movement — this second pass roughly doubled the topic's mock question count (18→32) by exhausting the remaining genuine syllabus-relevant material rather than padding with restatements of already-covered facts.

reading.intro: Fourth topic of Unit III, covering 19th-century reform movements and the earliest organized resistance to British rule (pre-1857). Real exam evidence favors founder-attribution and chronological-ordering questions.

qa.question: Real exam: Arrange chronologically — Civil Disobedience Movement, Sepoy Mutiny, Vellore Mutiny, South Indian Rebellion.
qa.answer: South Indian Rebellion → Vellore Mutiny (1806) → Sepoy Mutiny (1857) → Civil Disobedience Movement (1930) — in that chronological order.

qa.question: Real exam: Who said his idea was to establish 'a worship of the heart and not of the hand, a sacrifice of the self and not of the possessions of the self'?
qa.answer: Raja Ram Mohan Roy.

qa.question: Who founded the Brahmo Sabha (later Brahmo Samaj), and what was he popularly called?
qa.answer: Raja Ram Mohan Roy founded it (1828, Calcutta). He is popularly called the 'Morning Star of India' — often regarded as the father of the Indian Renaissance.

qa.question: What were Brahmo Samaj's core positions — and what is a common MIS-statement about it to watch for?
qa.answer: Brahmo Samaj rejected polytheism, idol worship, and divine incarnations, and condemned the caste system and superstitions. Watch the trap: Brahmo Samaj did NOT oppose widow remarriage — it actually supported social reforms including widow remarriage; any statement claiming it 'opposed' widow remarriage is false.

qa.question: In what year was Sati (widow immolation) formally abolished by Act, and who founded the Arya Samaj?
qa.answer: Sati was abolished in 1829 (under Lord William Bentinck, with Raja Ram Mohan Roy's strong support). Arya Samaj was founded in 1875 by Swami Dayananda Saraswati.

qa.question: What was Dayananda Saraswati's famous slogan, and which book did he write?
qa.answer: 'Go back to the Vedas.' He wrote Satyarth Prakash.

qa.question: Who founded the Ramakrishna Mission, and when?
qa.answer: Swami Vivekananda, in 1897.

qa.question: Who passed the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act of 1856 — and what's worth double-checking about this fact?
qa.answer: One source attributes it to Lord Dalhousie, but the Act was actually passed (26 July 1856) after Dalhousie's term as Governor-General had ended (Feb 1856) — Lord Canning had already taken over. Treat 'Canning' as the more historically defensible answer, but be aware some TNPSC-prep material states 'Dalhousie' instead.

qa.question: What did T.B. Macaulay intend to create through English education in India?
qa.answer: A class of people 'Indian in blood and colour, but English in taste, opinion, morals, and intellect' — i.e., an English-educated Indian elite to help administer British rule.

qa.question: What role did Christian missionaries play in modern Indian education?
qa.answer: They were among the earliest providers of modern education in India, AND they gave educational opportunities to underprivileged sections who were denied traditional (caste-restricted) learning — both statements are true.

qa.question: What was the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha, who led it, and what did it protest?
qa.answer: Led by Mahadev Govind Ranade, it awakened political consciousness among people and protested the Vernacular Press Act of 1878. (It did NOT publish the Amrita Bazar Patrika — that's a false statement to watch for.)

qa.question: Who founded the Deccan Education Society, and who established the Hindu Dharma Pravardhini Sabha (and when)?
qa.answer: Deccan Education Society — G.G. Agarkar. Hindu Dharma Pravardhini Sabha — Madan Mohan Malaviya, 1906.

qa.question: What was the first political challenge taken up by the Women's India Association?
qa.answer: The right to vote.

qa.question: What social evil did British administrators Colonel Todd, Duncan, and Malcolm discuss and act against?
qa.answer: Female infanticide.

qa.question: Who founded the Prarthana Samaj, and when/where?
qa.answer: Dr. Atmaram Pandurang founded it in 1867 in Bombay — a reformist Hindu organization close in spirit to the Brahmo Samaj, later closely associated with Mahadev Govind Ranade (the same Ranade who led the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha).

qa.question: Who founded the Theosophical Society, when, and what is its Indian connection?
qa.answer: Founded in 1875 in New York by Madame H.P. Blavatsky and Colonel H.S. Olcott. It later moved its international headquarters to Adyar, Madras (Chennai) — and Annie Besant, who joined the society, became its president in 1907, going on to also become the first woman president of the Indian National Congress (1917).

qa.question: Who led the Aligarh Movement, and what was its central aim?
qa.answer: Sir Syed Ahmed Khan, who founded the Mohammedan Anglo-Oriental (MAO) College at Aligarh in 1875 to promote modern, English-medium education among Indian Muslims. The MAO College later became Aligarh Muslim University in 1920.
qa.tip: Watch the founder-name trap: Sir Syed Ahmed Khan (Aligarh, Muslim modern-education reform) is a completely different person from Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan (the later 'Frontier Gandhi') — don't confuse the two similarly-titled 'Khan' reformers.

qa.question: What was the Young Bengal Movement, and who led it?
qa.answer: A radical, rationalist student movement at Hindu College, Calcutta, in the late 1820s-early 1830s, led by the young Anglo-Indian teacher Henry Louis Vivian Derozio. It sharply attacked orthodox Hindu customs and superstition and encouraged free thought, though it had limited lasting mass impact.

qa.question: Who founded the Satyashodhak Samaj, and what were his key social reforms?
qa.answer: Jyotiba Phule founded the Satyashodhak Samaj ('Truth-Seekers' Society') in 1873 in Maharashtra, campaigning against caste oppression and Brahmanical dominance. He also opened one of India's first girls' schools in Pune (1848, with his wife Savitribai Phule) and pioneered support for widow remarriage in western India.

qa.question: What was Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar's specific role in the widow remarriage reform, beyond just 'supporting' it?
qa.answer: He was the key campaigner and petitioner behind the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act of 1856 — using Sanskrit textual scholarship to argue widow remarriage was sanctioned by Hindu tradition, not opposed to it. He also founded/promoted girls' schools, extending the era's reform agenda beyond Bengal's Brahmo circles.

qa.question: Name two Tamil Nadu-specific 19th-century social reformers (outside the national Brahmo/Arya Samaj movements) and their contributions.
qa.answer: Ramalinga Adigal (Vallalar) founded the Samarasa Suddha Sanmarga Sangam (1865/67), preaching universal compassion and opposing caste distinctions. Vaikunda Swamigal preached against caste oppression and untouchability in southern Tamil Nadu in the early-to-mid 19th century, founding what later became known as the Ayyavazhi tradition.

qa.question: What was the Singh Sabha Movement, and what lasting institution did it establish?
qa.answer: A Sikh reform movement started in 1873 at Amritsar (with another influential branch at Lahore from 1879), aimed at reviving Sikh teachings and countering both Hindu revivalist and Christian missionary influence among Sikhs. It established Khalsa College, Amritsar, in 1892.

qa.question: Round-2 pass (2026-07): What was the Wahabi Movement, who led it, and how did British authorities treat it?
qa.answer: A religio-political movement started by Syed Ahmed Barelvi (Raebareli) in the early 19th century, aiming to purify Islam and, after his death fighting the Sikhs at Balakot (1831), evolving under his followers (based mainly around Patna, Bihar) into armed and covert resistance against British rule through the mid-to-late 1800s. British authorities treated it as a serious internal-security threat, prosecuting a series of 'Wahabi Trials' in the 1860s-70s.

qa.question: What was the Faraizi Movement, and who were its two key leaders?
qa.answer: A religious-reform-turned-agrarian movement among Muslim peasants of East Bengal, founded by Haji Shariatullah (calling on Muslims to return to the 'fara'iz', the obligatory duties of Islam) and carried into militant anti-zamindar, anti-indigo-planter resistance by his son Dudu Mian, who organized peasants to resist illegal taxes and land oppression.

qa.question: What was the Kuka Movement, who founded it, and how did the British finally suppress it?
qa.answer: A Sikh revivalist ('Namdhari') movement founded around 1840 by Bhagat Jawahar Mal ('Sian Saheb') in western Punjab, later led by Baba Ram Singh; it began as a religious purification/cow-protection campaign and turned increasingly anti-British. The British suppressed it harshly, most notoriously by blowing 65-plus Kuka activists from cannon at Malerkotla in January 1872 without trial.

qa.question: What was the Santhal Rebellion (Hul), when did it occur, who led it, and what lasting administrative change did it produce?
qa.answer: An 1855-56 armed uprising by the Santhal tribe of the Rajmahal Hills (present-day Jharkhand/Bengal-Bihar border) against oppressive zamindars, moneylenders, and British revenue officials, led by the brothers Sidhu and Kanhu Murmu. It was crushed by British troops, but the colonial government subsequently carved out the separate Santhal Parganas district (1855) to administer the area under special tenancy protections.

qa.question: Who was Titu Mir, and what is he remembered for in early anti-British resistance?
qa.answer: Mir Nisar Ali, known as Titu Mir, led armed Muslim peasants and tenants of Bengal (with Faraizi/Wahabi sympathies) against oppressive Hindu zamindars and British indigo planters in 1831. He built a fortified 'bamboo fort' (Bansher Kella) at Narkelberia, which British artillery ultimately destroyed, killing Titu Mir — often cited as one of the earliest instances of organized armed peasant resistance to British-backed exploitation.

qa.question: What was the Kol Uprising (1831-32), and which tribal groups were involved?
qa.answer: An armed uprising of the Ho and Munda (Kol) tribal groups of Chhotanagpur (present Jharkhand) against the imposition of outside (non-tribal) landlords, moneylenders, and revenue officials on their traditional lands — one of several early tribal revolts (alongside the later Santhal Hul) against colonial land-revenue intrusion into tribal areas.

qa.question: What was the Paika Rebellion (1817), and why do some historians call it significant beyond just a local revolt?
qa.answer: An armed uprising in Odisha led by Buxi Jagabandhu, in which the Paikas (a traditional militia/landholding warrior class who had lost their rent-free lands under British land-revenue settlement) rose against East India Company rule. Some historians argue it deserves recognition as one of the earliest large-scale armed rebellions against British rule, predating 1857 by four decades.

qa.question: What was the Kittur Rebellion (1824), and who led it?
qa.answer: Rani Chennamma of Kittur (in present-day Karnataka) led an armed revolt in 1824 after the British, invoking a lapse-of-succession-style doctrine, refused to recognize her adopted son's right to the throne and moved to annex the state — one of the earliest instances of armed resistance by an Indian ruler to British annexation policy, well before the Rani of Jhansi's more famous 1857 resistance.

qa.question: What was the Veda Samaj, when and where was it founded, and by whom?
qa.answer: Founded in Madras in 1864, modelled closely on the Brahmo Samaj, by Keshab Chandra Sen (during a visit to Madras) together with K. Sridharalu Naidu. It promoted the abolition of caste distinctions, widow remarriage, and girls' education in South India — a direct precursor to the later Brahmo-influenced reform current in the Madras Presidency.

qa.question: Who was Pandita Ramabai, and what institutions did she found for women?
qa.answer: A pioneering woman social reformer and scholar (honoured with the title 'Pandita' for her Sanskrit learning) who founded the Arya Mahila Samaj in Pune (1882) and later the Sharada Sadan, a home and school for widows and destitute women, extending the reform agenda specifically to child-widows' education and welfare — a rare female-led counterpart to the male-dominated reform societies of the era.

qa.question: Real exam (2024): Which pair of 'Revolt — Area' is WRONGLY matched among: (I) Ramosis—Poona, (II) Kuka—Punjab, (III) Santhals—Orissa, (IV) Kittur—Malabar?
qa.answer: Both (III) and (IV) are wrongly matched: the Santhal Rebellion was centred on the Rajmahal Hills (Bengal-Bihar border, in today's Jharkhand), not Orissa; and the Kittur Rebellion was in present-day Karnataka, not Malabar. (I) Ramosis—Poona and (II) Kuka—Punjab are correctly matched.
qa.tip: For 'revolt + area' matching questions, memorize the actual region for each named revolt individually rather than guessing by a state's general 'unrest' reputation — Santhals and Kittur are the two TNPSC most often mismatches deliberately.

qa.question: What was the Deoband Movement, when and by whom was its seminary founded, and how did it differ in aim from the Wahabi Movement?
qa.answer: The Darul Uloom Deoband seminary was founded in 1866 at Deoband (present-day Uttar Pradesh) by Muhammad Qasim Nanautavi and Maulana Rashid Ahmad Gangohi, focusing on reviving Islamic scholarship and religious education through a network of Islamic schools, rather than the more overtly armed/political resistance associated with the Wahabi Movement — though both currents shared a broadly anti-British, Islam-revivalist outlook.

questions.prompt: Arrange chronologically (earliest first): Sepoy Mutiny, Civil Disobedience Movement, Vellore Mutiny, South Indian Rebellion.
options: South Indian Rebellion, Vellore Mutiny, Sepoy Mutiny, Civil Disobedience Movement
Vellore Mutiny, South Indian Rebellion, Sepoy Mutiny, Civil Disobedience Movement
Sepoy Mutiny, Vellore Mutiny, South Indian Rebellion, Civil Disobedience Movement
Civil Disobedience Movement, Sepoy Mutiny, Vellore Mutiny, South Indian Rebellion
questions.explanation: South Indian Rebellion → Vellore Mutiny (1806) → Sepoy Mutiny (1857) → Civil Disobedience Movement (1930).

questions.prompt: Who said his goal was 'a worship of the heart and not of the hand, a sacrifice of the self and not of the possessions of the self'?
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy.

questions.prompt: Raja Ram Mohan Roy, founder of the Brahmo Sabha, is popularly known as the:
questions.explanation: Morning Star of India.

questions.prompt: Which of the following did Brahmo Samaj actually OPPOSE or REJECT?
questions.explanation: Idol worship/polytheism — Brahmo Samaj supported widow remarriage and social reform, it didn't oppose them.

questions.prompt: Sati was formally abolished by Act in which year?
Arya Samaj's famous slogan, coined by its founder Swami Dayananda Saraswati, was:
questions.explanation: Go back to the Vedas.

questions.prompt: The Ramakrishna Mission was founded in 1897 by:
questions.explanation: Swami Vivekananda.

questions.prompt: T.B. Macaulay's vision for English education in India aimed to create a class of people who were:
options: Fluent only in Sanskrit and Persian
Indian in blood and colour, but English in taste, opinion, morals and intellect
questions.explanation: Macaulay's well-known formulation.

questions.prompt: The Poona Sarvajanik Sabha, led by M.G. Ranade, protested which legislative act in 1878?
questions.explanation: The Vernacular Press Act, 1878.

questions.prompt: The first political challenge taken up by the Women's India Association was the right to:
questions.explanation: The right to vote.

questions.prompt: The Prarthana Samaj was founded in 1867 in Bombay by:
questions.explanation: Dr. Atmaram Pandurang (M.G. Ranade was later closely associated with it, but was not its founder).

questions.prompt: The Theosophical Society, founded in 1875 in New York by Blavatsky and Olcott, later established its international headquarters at:
questions.explanation: Adyar, Madras (Chennai).

questions.prompt: Sir Syed Ahmed Khan founded the MAO College at Aligarh in 1875, which later (1920) became known as:
questions.explanation: Aligarh Muslim University.

questions.prompt: The radical Young Bengal Movement at Hindu College, Calcutta, was led by:
questions.explanation: Henry Louis Vivian Derozio.

questions.prompt: Jyotiba Phule founded the Satyashodhak Samaj in 1873 in which region, and for what purpose?
questions.explanation: Maharashtra, campaigning against caste oppression and Brahmanical dominance.

questions.prompt: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar's key reform contribution was:
options: Campaigning for the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act, 1856
questions.explanation: He was the key campaigner behind the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act, 1856.

questions.prompt: Ramalinga Adigal (Vallalar), who founded the Samarasa Suddha Sanmarga Sangam preaching against caste distinctions, was from which state?
questions.explanation: Tamil Nadu.

questions.prompt: The Singh Sabha Movement (Sikh reform), started in 1873, established which lasting educational institution in 1892?
questions.explanation: Khalsa College, Amritsar.

questions.prompt: The Wahabi Movement, started by Syed Ahmed Barelvi, was based mainly around which region for its later anti-British activity?
questions.explanation: Patna, Bihar — the base for the movement's later covert/armed resistance, leading to a series of British 'Wahabi Trials'.

questions.prompt: The Faraizi Movement's shift from religious reform to militant anti-zamindar, anti-planter peasant resistance in East Bengal is chiefly credited to:
options: Dudu Mian, son of Haji Shariatullah
questions.explanation: Dudu Mian organized peasants into militant resistance against illegal taxes and land oppression, building on his father Haji Shariatullah's religious reform base.

questions.prompt: The Kuka (Namdhari) Movement's founder, who began it as a Sikh religious purification campaign around 1840, was:
questions.explanation: Bhagat Jawahar Mal founded the movement; Baba Ram Singh later led it into more overt anti-British politics.
questions.trick: Founder vs. later leader trap: Bhagat Jawahar Mal FOUNDED the Kuka Movement; Baba Ram Singh is the leader most associated with its later anti-British turn and the 1872 Malerkotla executions.

questions.prompt: In January 1872, the British suppressed Kuka activists by:
options: Blowing over 65 of them from cannon at Malerkotla without trial
questions.explanation: The Malerkotla cannon executions (Jan 1872) are the movement's most infamous suppression event.

questions.prompt: The Santhal Rebellion (Hul) of 1855-56 was led by the brothers:
questions.explanation: Sidhu and Kanhu Murmu led the Santhal tribe's uprising in the Rajmahal Hills.

questions.prompt: The direct administrative outcome of the Santhal Rebellion was the British creation of:
options: The Chota Nagpur Tenancy Act only
Direct Crown rule replacing Company rule
questions.explanation: The Santhal Parganas district was carved out in 1855 with special tenancy protections for the tribal area.

questions.prompt: Titu Mir (Mir Nisar Ali) is remembered for building which structure as a base for his 1831 armed resistance in Bengal?
options: A bamboo fort (Bansher Kella) at Narkelberia
An underground tunnel network near Calcutta
questions.explanation: His 'Bansher Kella' (bamboo fort) at Narkelberia was ultimately destroyed by British artillery, killing Titu Mir.

questions.prompt: The Kol Uprising of 1831-32 involved which tribal groups, and in which region?
questions.explanation: Ho and Munda (Kol) tribes rose against outside landlords/moneylenders imposed on Chhotanagpur's tribal lands.

questions.prompt: The Paika Rebellion of 1817, led by Buxi Jagabandhu, occurred in which region and arose chiefly from which grievance?
options: Odisha; loss of the Paikas' traditional rent-free militia lands under British revenue settlement
Punjab; religious persecution of Sikh militia
questions.explanation: The Paikas, a warrior-militia landholding class in Odisha, lost their traditional rent-free lands under Company revenue policy and rose in armed revolt in 1817 — decades before 1857.

questions.prompt: Rani Chennamma's 1824 armed revolt at Kittur was triggered chiefly by:
options: British refusal to recognize her adopted son's succession and moving to annex the state
questions.explanation: The British invoked a lapse-of-succession-style doctrine to deny her adopted son's claim and moved to annex Kittur, provoking her armed resistance — well before the more famous 1857-era Rani of Jhansi episode.

questions.prompt: The Veda Samaj, founded in Madras in 1864 by Keshab Chandra Sen and K. Sridharalu Naidu, was modelled closely on:
questions.explanation: It was a direct South Indian extension of Brahmo Samaj ideals — anti-caste, pro widow-remarriage, pro girls' education.

questions.prompt: Pandita Ramabai founded the Arya Mahila Samaj (1882, Pune) and later the Sharada Sadan, focusing her reform efforts especially on:
options: Trade union rights for factory workers
Education and welfare of child-widows and destitute women
questions.explanation: Sharada Sadan was specifically a home and school for widows and destitute women.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2024): Which of the following 'Revolt — Area' pairs is correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Ramosis—Poona is correct; the Santhal Rebellion was centred on the Rajmahal Hills (not Orissa), Kittur is in Karnataka (not Malabar), and the Kuka Movement was based in Punjab (not Bengal).
questions.trick: When a 'match the pairs' question flips which pair is correct vs. wrong across different exam sittings, re-derive each pair from the actual historical region rather than pattern-matching against a remembered answer key.

questions.prompt: The Darul Uloom Deoband seminary, founded in 1866, differed from the Wahabi Movement mainly in that it:
options: Focused on reviving Islamic scholarship/education through schools rather than armed resistance
Was founded specifically to support British rule
Was led by Syed Ahmed Barelvi himself
questions.explanation: Deoband (Muhammad Qasim Nanautavi and Rashid Ahmad Gangohi) pursued religious-educational revival rather than the more overtly armed resistance associated with the Wahabi Movement.
